title: T5.4: Implement ternary zero regularizer
description: Soft L1-style penalty on the pre-threshold expert weights, encouraging sparsity (more zeros in the ternary representation). This is useful for SparseTernaryBitplanes encoding where higher zero density means fewer bytes. For Ternary2, zeros don't save bytes but they do reduce accumulator magnitude, which is still useful. Config-gated.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::sparsity passes; All-zero weights -> regularizer=0; Near-zero weights penalized

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/loss/sparsity.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "ternary zero regularizer", "L1", "sparsity".

### Implementation Notes
- Soft L1-style penalty on pre-threshold expert weights, encouraging sparsity (more zeros in ternary encoding).
- `pub fn ternary_zero_regularizer(weights: &Tensor, threshold: &Tensor) -> Tensor`
- Penalty: `Σ |w| * (1 - sigmoid(alpha * (|w| - threshold)))` — smooth version that penalizes weights near threshold
- Or simpler: `Σ min(|w|, threshold)` — encourages weights to be exactly 0 or above threshold
- Config-gated: only active when `lambda_zero > 0.0`.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::sparsity       # Tests pass
# Verify: all-zero weights -> regularizer = 0
# Verify: penalty increases with number of near-zero weights
# Verify: gradient encourages weights toward 0 or ±scale
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Pure function
- §I.5: Differentiable w.r.t. weights — honest gradient

title: T7.3: Implement temporal smoothness regularization in router
description: In Top1RouterQat, maintain a running buffer of the previous token's soft routing distribution within each training sequence. Compute L_switch = (1/LT) sum_{l,t} (1 - dot(p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1})) over contiguous windows. Handle sequence boundaries (mask out cross-sequence penalties). Multiply by lambda_switch. Gradient flows through the router's softmax output. Window size is a config parameter (default: 32 tokens).
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router::temporal passes; Buffer resets at sequence boundaries; Smoothness in [0,1]

title: T10.14: ExportVisitor-to-ArtifactOracle agreement test
description:
Tests that the exported artifact, when evaluated by ArtifactOracle, produces the same outputs as the training model's fake-quant forward pass.

**Forward agreement test:**
- Create tiny model, run forward pass on 4 fixture prompts, record logits L_train
- Export to ModelArtifact
- Load artifact into ArtifactOracle (exact canonical evaluation, no tiling/banking)
- Run ArtifactOracle on same 4 prompts, record logits L_artifact
- ASSERT: L_train == L_artifact (exact equality, not approximate — fake-quant forward should match)

**Checkpoint-level agreement:**
- At each SemanticCheckpointId (PostEmbedding, PostRouter, PostExpertDowncast, PostLogits, PostDecode):
  - Record intermediate values from training forward pass
  - Record intermediate values from ArtifactOracle
  - ASSERT: exact match

**Edge case: different decode modes:**
- Argmax decode: verify token output matches between training model and oracle
- TopKTemperature decode: verify logit distributions match (tokens may differ due to RNG, but pre-sample distributions must agree)

**Quantization gap measurement:**
- Evaluate dense teacher on fixture prompts -> logits L_teacher
- Evaluate ternary artifact on same prompts -> logits L_artifact
- Record: max abs difference, mean abs difference, KL divergence
- Log these as structured metrics for conformance.json
- These are NOT assertions (some gap is expected) — they're measurements

This test is the training-side companion to the oracle suite's differential tests.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- oracle_agreement passes; Forward agreement within atol=1e-5; Checkpoint-level values match; Quantization gap measured

title: F12: Dual-Path Sequence-State Experimentation — LinearState + BoundedKv
description: The plan keeps both LinearState (recurrent/linear-attention) and BoundedKv as production candidates. gbf-model abstracts over SequenceSemanticsSpec; both blocks are implemented under the same training scaffold (phase scheduler, loss, QAT modules) and run through shadow_compile so the production choice is decided empirically on workloads, not in spec docs. Production CompileProfiles for each variant are defined only after this experiment resolves.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F12: Dual-Path Sequence-State Experimentation

### Background

planv0.md keeps two production candidates open: `SequenceSemanticsSpec::LinearState` (recurrent / linear-attention-like) and `SequenceSemanticsSpec::BoundedKv` (causal bounded-KV). Earlier framings tried to declare one a winner. The 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session committed to the opposite: **decide empirically**. Both must be implementable on the same training scaffold so the comparison is apples-to-apples.

### Why this matters

The runtime memory math is very different between the two:
- **LinearState**: state is small, fits in WRAM ping-pong, no growth with context. SRAM persistence is a checkpoint, not a working set.
- **BoundedKv**: KV slab grows with context; even at small contexts it likely exceeds WRAM, so SRAM paging through `SramPagePlan` is required, with hot tiles streamed into WRAM.

Without F12, the trainer has to pick one before any data exists. With F12, both paths run through the same QAT phases, the same loss terms, the same shadow_compile pipeline, and produce side-by-side `CheckpointFrontierPoint` data. The production `CompileProfile` (`Production-LinearState` or `Production-BoundedKv`) is defined only after T12.4's `SequenceStateComparisonReport` is in.

### The problem this solves

- Eliminates premature commitment that would force a costly later rework.
- Forces the model code to abstract over sequence semantics from day one (this is good architecture; it would be the right shape even if only one variant shipped).
- Gives F11's `CompileProfile.sequence_state` field a concrete enum to refer to.
- Aligns shadow_compile (F8) with the experimentation goal: the same compile pipeline runs both variants on the same workloads.

### Design

`gbf-model` owns `SequenceSemanticsSpec` and a `SequenceBlock` trait:

```rust
pub enum SequenceSemanticsSpec {
    LinearState { state_bytes_per_layer: u16 },
    BoundedKv { max_context: u16, kv_bytes_per_token: u16 },
}

pub trait SequenceBlock {
    fn forward(&self, x: ActivationTile, state: &mut SequenceState) -> ActivationTile;
    fn state_init(&self) -> SequenceState;
    fn state_size(&self) -> SequenceStateSize;
    fn export_facts(&self) -> SequenceExportFacts;
}
```

`ArtifactCore` (in `gbf-artifact`) carries `SequenceSemanticsSpec` directly so artifacts of different sequence-state types are distinguishable.

The training scaffold (`gbf-train`): phase scheduler, loss terms (z-loss, balance, range, switch, distillation), QAT modules (ternary, activation, norm), and shadow_compile all dispatch through `SequenceBlock`. No code outside the block dispatch branches on the variant.

### Confidence: HIGH for the trait/abstraction; MEDIUM for which variant wins

The abstraction is independently good architecture. The actual outcome of LinearState vs BoundedKv depends on workloads we don't have yet — that's exactly what T12.4's report measures.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T12.1–T12.4.

**Target crate(s)**: `gbf-model` (types + blocks), `gbf-artifact` (SequenceSemanticsSpec lands in ArtifactCore), `gbf-train` (scaffold dispatch).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "SequenceSemanticsSpec", "LinearState", "BoundedKv", "fixed-state recurrent", "bounded-KV causal".

### Feature Definition of Done

All child tasks (T12.1–T12.4) closed AND:
```bash
cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence_block               # Both blocks pass shared trait tests
cargo test -p gbf-model -- linear_state                 # LinearState-specific
cargo test -p gbf-model -- bounded_kv                   # BoundedKv-specific
cargo test -p gbf-train -- shadow_compile::dual_path    # Both variants run shadow_compile on a tiny model
cargo test -p gbf-test -- sequence_state_comparison_report  # Report shape is stable
```

title: T10.7: Phase transition integration tests (F4 coverage)
description:
Integration tests for the 5-phase training schedule:

**Phase transition correctness:**
- Create tiny model, configure 5-phase schedule with step boundaries [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
- Step through phases, verify at each boundary:
  - QAT hardness changes to expected values per TrainPhaseSpec
  - Loss term activation changes (λ_balance/λ_zrouter enabled from Phase B, λ_distill from Phase C, etc.)
  - Correct log events are emitted (phase_transition event with before/after hardness)
- Verify model behavior changes: Phase A forward should not ternary-quantize; Phase C forward should

**Teacher freeze and validation:**
- Train for 10 steps (Phase A), freeze teacher
- Verify teacher weights are frozen (no gradient)
- Verify teacher weights != student weights after 10 more steps of QAT
- Export teacher as ReferenceModelBundle, verify it passes ArtifactValidation
- Verify teacher and student produce different but correlated outputs (distillation should work)

**Phase scheduler edge cases:**
- Empty phase list: verify error
- Overlapping phases: verify error
- Skip a phase (e.g., go directly to Phase C): verify correct hardness applied
- Single-step phases: verify transitions fire correctly

**Logging verification:**
- Capture all training events via test logging harness
- Verify: exactly one phase_transition event per boundary
- Verify: loss_step events contain all expected fields with correct types
- Verify: export events contain artifact_core_hash field

All tests use tiny model fixture from T10.1.

title: T2.5: runtime_nucleus_hash CI drift gate between training and production shell
description: CI step that: (1) on every PR touching gbf-runtime, re-emits RuntimeChromeBudget; (2) compares runtime_nucleus_hash to the pinned training-time hash recorded in the active training run manifest (or the canonical reference budget); (3) fails the PR unless the bump is intentional and tagged with a release note that promotes the new hash. Trainer also self-checks at every step that the captured pin is unchanged.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target**: CI workflow + `gbf-runtime` + `gbf-train`.
**Target file(s)**: `.github/workflows/runtime-nucleus-drift.yml` (new) or equivalent CI config; `gbf-train/src/preflight.rs` (self-check); a reusable `tools/check-nucleus-drift` binary if needed.

**Reference**: bd-1hv 2026-04-26 session comment ("`runtime_nucleus_hash` is load-bearing"), T2.1 refinement (bd-yfv), T2.4 (bd-37r).

### Implementation Notes

Two layers of drift detection, both required:

1. **CI gate (production-shell drift).**
   - Trigger: any PR that modifies `gbf-runtime/`, `gbf-policy/src/budget.rs`, `gbf-policy/src/profile.rs`, `gbf-policy/src/reference_shell.rs`, or anything that ends up in the runtime-nucleus binary.
   - Action: re-emit `RuntimeChromeBudget` from the post-PR shell. Compare `runtime_nucleus_hash` to the value pinned in `artifacts/calibration/pinned_reference_budget.json` (or whatever path the project chooses).
   - Failure mode: PR fails with a diagnostic listing both hashes and per-slot byte deltas. The PR author either (a) reverts the budget-affecting change, or (b) explicitly bumps the pin in the same PR with a release note that promotes the new hash.

2. **Trainer self-check (active-run drift).**
   - At training start, the trainer captures the pinned `runtime_nucleus_hash` into the run manifest.
   - At every checkpoint commit and every export step, the trainer re-reads the active `RuntimeChromeBudget` (synthetic or real) and asserts the hash is unchanged.
   - On drift: trainer halts with a clear error pointing to (i) the pinned hash, (ii) the current hash, (iii) which file change likely caused it (via `git log -p <budget_file>` summary).

### Release-note convention for promoting a new hash

Promotion is a deliberate, reviewable act. The mechanism:
- A dedicated marker file `artifacts/calibration/PINNED_HASH_HISTORY.md` records every pinned hash with date, the modules included, and the reason for promotion.
- CI reads the most recent entry's hash; PRs that change the runtime nucleus must add a new entry to be merged.

### What this task does NOT do

It does not specify the byte-level encoding of `runtime_nucleus_hash` (that's T2.1 / bd-yfv). It does not implement the synthetic budget (that's T2.6 / bd-ros). It composes those.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
# Locally:
cargo run -p tools-check-nucleus-drift                         # Exit 0 when in sync
cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight::nucleus_self_check        # Trainer self-check enforces drift

# CI:
# Verify: a PR adding a new file to gbf-runtime/src/ that ends up in the nucleus fails CI without a pin promotion
# Verify: a PR that promotes the pin (updates PINNED_HASH_HISTORY.md) passes
# Verify: the diagnostic on failure lists both hashes and the offending file paths
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.4: CI gate is the verifiable reward — exit 0 means no silent drift.
- §IV.3: Reproducible budget — same source produces same hash; CI catches deviations.
- §V.3: Loud failure with structured diff (old hash, new hash, byte deltas) on drift.
- §VI.1: PINNED_HASH_HISTORY.md is the single source of truth for hash promotion.

title: T10.15: Full regression test script with diagnostic summary
description:
A single entry-point script (cargo test -p gbf-test -- --training-contract or a dedicated binary) that runs ALL training-contract tests in the correct order and produces a structured diagnostic summary.

**Test execution order:**
1. Infrastructure: tiny model fixtures, logging setup
2. Unit tests: QAT modules, byte math, model config, loss terms, router, budget, Pareto
3. Integration tests: phase transitions, temporal smoothness boundaries
4. E2E tests: train-to-export, export determinism, oracle agreement, shadow compile
5. Regression checks: compare against golden values from last known-good run

**Output artifacts:**
- test_results.json: per-test pass/fail/skip with timing, failure messages, and structured diagnostics
- test_summary.txt: human-readable one-line-per-test with [PASS]/[FAIL]/[SKIP] and total counts
- training_metrics.json: loss curves from E2E training test (useful for detecting training regressions)
- artifact_hashes.json: content hashes of all exported artifacts (useful for detecting export regressions)
- byte_counts.json: per-expert byte counts (useful for detecting budget formula regressions)

**CI integration:**
- Exit code 0 if all tests pass, 1 if any fail
- Failed test output includes: test name, assertion that failed, expected vs actual, full structured context
- Summary line: 'Training-contract tests: 47/47 passed, 0 failed, 0 skipped (12.3s)'
- Optionally: upload test_results.json and training_metrics.json as CI artifacts

**Golden value management:**
- Golden values stored in fixtures/golden/training_contract/
- When REGENERATE_GOLDEN=1, tests overwrite golden values instead of comparing
- Golden values include: artifact hashes, byte counts, phase transition sequences, loss term values for known inputs

**Performance guard:**
- Total test suite should complete in < 60 seconds on CI (tiny model, CPU only)
- Individual slow tests (E2E) are marked with a time budget and warn if exceeded
acceptance_criteria: ./scripts/test_all.sh exits 0; test_results.json lists ALL tests; Total runtime <60s; Golden hash comparison works; Exit 1 on any failure

title: T10.10: Pareto frontier selection correctness tests (F8 coverage)
description:
Unit tests for Pareto frontier tracking and checkpoint selection:

**Dominance relation tests:**
- Point A (quality=0.9, fit=true, cost=100) dominates B (quality=0.8, fit=true, cost=150): verify A dominates B
- Point A (quality=0.9, fit=true, cost=100) vs C (quality=0.85, fit=true, cost=80): verify neither dominates (Pareto-incomparable)
- Point with fit=false is never selected regardless of quality/cost

**Frontier tracking tests:**
- Insert 5 known points, verify frontier contains exactly the non-dominated ones
- Verify frontier size never exceeds keep_frontier limit
- Verify oldest non-frontier points are evicted first when at capacity

**Selection algorithm tests:**
- Given frontier: [(quality=0.9,cost=100), (quality=0.85,cost=80), (quality=0.95,cost=200)]
- After filtering fit=true, verify selected = highest quality point (0.95, cost=200)
- If best-quality point has fit=false, verify next-best is selected
- If all points have fit=false: verify selection returns None with appropriate error

**training_selection.json output:**
- Run selection on known frontier, verify JSON contains: frontier array, selected checkpoint_id, domination relationships, timestamp
- Verify JSON is valid and parseable

**Edge cases:**
- Single point frontier: verify it's selected
- All identical quality: verify any one is selected (deterministic tiebreak)
- Empty frontier: verify error, not crash
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- pareto_tests passes; Dominance is transitive; Frontier bounded; Selection never picks hard-failed checkpoint

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train` (export pipeline) + relies on T2.3 (bd-27n) preflight.
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/export.rs` (extend or new), `gbf-train/src/shadow.rs` (re-validation hook).

**Reference**: bd-1hv 2026-04-26 session ("Per-export re-validation"), T2.3 refinement (bd-27n), T2.5 (bd-177).

### Implementation Notes

Add an explicit re-validation stage to the export pipeline that runs the same preflight from T2.3 against the **current** `RuntimeChromeBudget` (not the training-time pinned one).

1. **Inputs.**
   - The just-exported `ArtifactCore` + lowering artifacts.
   - The training-time pinned `RuntimeChromeBudget` (from the run manifest).
   - The current production `RuntimeChromeBudget` (re-emitted from the live shell).

2. **Action.**
   - Compare the two `runtime_nucleus_hash`es. If equal, skip the byte-delta comparison and run the standard export-time preflight against the pinned budget.
   - If they differ:
     - Compute per-slot byte deltas.
     - Re-run the preflight against the **current** budget (this is the authoritative check for what production will actually receive).
     - Emit `BudgetDriftReport`:
       ```rust
       pub struct BudgetDriftReport {
           pub training_hash: Hash256,
           pub current_hash: Hash256,
           pub training_modules: BTreeSet<RuntimeShellModule>,
           pub current_modules: BTreeSet<RuntimeShellModule>,
           pub slot_deltas: Vec<RomBudgetSlotDelta>,
           pub wram_delta: WramReservedDelta,
           pub sram_delta: i32,
       }
       ```
   - If `fits_envelope == false` against the current budget, **block export**. Hard failure.
   - If only `risk_flags` fire, surface them but do not block.

3. **Diagnostic.**

   ```
   Budget drift detected:
     Training-time:  runtime_nucleus_hash=0xAB12...  modules={Boot,…,VideoCommit}
     At export:      runtime_nucleus_hash=0xCD34...  modules={Boot,…,VideoCommit,Persistence}
     ExpertBank.usable_bytes: 15488 -> 15104 (delta -384, persistence module added).
     Re-running preflight against current budget...
     Expert payload demand=14976 bytes. PASS (margin 128 bytes).
   ```

4. **Where it runs.**
   - Phase E (HardenAndSelect) export pipeline.
   - F8 shadow_compile pipeline (so drift is caught early during training, not only at final export).

### Coordination with T2.5

T2.5 prevents the production shell from drifting silently. T2.7 handles the case where drift is intentional and the trainer needs to react. They are complementary, not redundant.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- export::revalidation_no_drift        # Same hash: standard preflight runs
cargo test -p gbf-train -- export::revalidation_drift_passing   # Drift but fits: warning, export proceeds
cargo test -p gbf-train -- export::revalidation_drift_blocking  # Drift and breaks fit: export blocked
cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings                        # No warnings
# Verify: BudgetDriftReport is emitted as JSON to artifacts/runs/<run_id>/budget_drift.json
# Verify: blocked export returns a non-zero exit code with a structured error
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.4: Re-validation is the verifiable reward at export — exit 0 means production fit is confirmed.
- §V.1: BudgetDriftReport is JSON.
- §V.3: Diagnostic on block lists both hashes, modules, slot deltas, and the offending preflight failure.
- §III.1: Re-validation runs at shadow_compile too, so drift is caught well before final export.

title: F7: Router Switch-Awareness — Temporal Smoothness and Export Diagnostics
description: Make the router explicitly aware of bank-switch cost via temporal smoothness regularization, z-loss, expert dropout, and low-rank projection. Export TemporalSwitchDigest/ClipSaturationDigest/ExpertPayloadDigest in ExportFacts. Add ExpertSlotAffinity to CompilePreferences.
acceptance_criteria: All T7.x tasks closed; Digest types tested; Router features tested; Integration tests pass

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F7: Router Switch-Awareness — Temporal Smoothness and Export Diagnostics

### Background

On Game Boy with MBC5, a bank switch costs real cycles: the runtime must write to the MBC5 ROM bank register (at $2000-$3FFF), update the HRAM shadow, and potentially flush/restore any state that depended on the previous bank's data. Calibration measurements will quantify this cost precisely, but even the lower bound (a few dozen M-cycles per switch) means that high switch rates kill throughput.

The router doesn't know this. A standard MoE router optimizes for per-token quality, not for temporal coherence between consecutive tokens. Two adjacent tokens in the same sentence might route to completely different experts in the same layer, causing a bank switch every token.

### The temporal switch penalty

L_switch = (1/LT) Σ_{l,t} (1 - ⟨p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1}⟩)

This directly penalizes the inner product gap between consecutive routing distributions. When the same expert is chosen for consecutive tokens, the dot product is close to 1 and the penalty is near 0. When different experts are chosen, the penalty increases.

This is much more informative than a vague "locality" term because:
1. It directly models the deployment cost (bank switches)
2. It's differentiable through the router's softmax
3. It can be measured during training and compared against deployment measurements

### Export diagnostics

TemporalSwitchDigest records per-layer temporal switch statistics:
- same_expert_rate_q8_8: what fraction of consecutive tokens use the same expert (in Q8.8 fixed point)
- transition_mass: per expert-pair transition probability (top-K transitions that account for most mass)

ClipSaturationDigest records how often activations hit the clipping bounds (early warning for range issues).
ExpertPayloadDigest records the actual exported byte count per expert (for budget verification).
ExpertSlotAffinity in CompilePreferences: hints to the compiler about which experts should be co-located based on transition patterns.

### Low-rank router

The router projects d_model -> n_experts, where n_experts is typically 8-32. A full-rank router is d_model × n_experts parameters. A low-rank router factorizes this as d_model → r → n_experts where r << n_experts, saving common-bank bytes and providing implicit regularization. For d_model=128, n_experts=16, rank=4: full-rank is 2048 params, low-rank is 128*4 + 4*16 = 576 params — 3.5x smaller.

### z-loss and expert dropout

These are standard MoE training techniques, not novel to this project:
- z-loss penalizes large router logits before softmax, preventing confident but fragile routing decisions
- Expert dropout randomly drops expert outputs during training, forcing the model to not over-rely on any single expert

They're included here because they interact with the temporal switch penalty: z-loss keeps router distributions softer, which makes the temporal switch penalty more effective (softer distributions have more room for gradients to shift routing toward temporal coherence).

### Confidence: HIGH

The temporal switch penalty is the most directly useful contribution. The export diagnostics are straightforward. The z-loss/expert dropout/low-rank are standard practice. Mild reservation: the specific interaction between z-loss temperature and temporal switch lambda will require tuning.

title: T5.2: Implement router z-loss and expert load-balance loss
description: Standard MoE losses. z-loss = (1/N) sum log(sum exp(router_logits))^2 — penalizes large pre-softmax logits. Load-balance = N * sum(fraction_tokens_per_expert * average_gate_per_expert) — penalizes uneven expert utilization. These are well-studied and should be included from Phase B (RouterWarmup) onward, not deferred to M6.

## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/loss/router.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "z-loss", "expert load-balance loss", "standard MoE losses".

### Implementation Notes
- Two standard MoE loss terms:
  1. **Router z-loss**: `L_z = (1/B) Σ_i log(Σ_j exp(z_{i,j}))^2` — penalizes large router logits
  2. **Expert load-balance loss**: `L_balance = n_experts * Σ_j f_j * P_j` where f_j = fraction of tokens routed to expert j, P_j = mean routing probability for expert j
- Both included from Phase B (RouterWarmup) onward — these are NOT M6-deferred.
- `pub fn router_z_loss(logits: &Tensor) -> Tensor`
- `pub fn load_balance_loss(routing_probs: &Tensor, expert_assignments: &Tensor, n_experts: usize) -> Tensor`

comments.text:
## F2: RuntimeChromeBudget — Build-Specific Bank Capacity Contract

### Background

The plan repeatedly talks about "bank-sized experts" and "15.5 KiB budget" but this number is a hand-waved approximation. In reality, the free space in each bank class depends on the specific runtime shell build: how much Bank0 space the scheduler/ISR/UI/keyboard/text renderer/video commit queue consume, how much common-bank space embeddings/router/classifier/shared LUTs occupy, and how much expert-bank slack is reserved for stubs and metadata.

### The problem

Without RuntimeChromeBudget, the trainer has two bad options:
1. Use a static constant (e.g., 15.5 KiB) that's wrong for every actual build
2. Parse the build's map.json to reverse-engineer available space

Both are fragile. The first silently produces experts that don't fit. The second couples the trainer to the compiler's output format.

### Design

RuntimeChromeBudget is emitted as a structured artifact from the runtime/UI shell build. It contains:
- `target` and `profile` (so the trainer knows which build this is for)
- `runtime_nucleus_hash` (so stale budgets are detectable)
- `rom_slots`: a Vec of `RomBudgetSlot`, each describing a bank class (Bank0Free, CommonBank, ExpertBank) with `usable_bytes`, `reserved_slack`, and supported `placement_caps`
- `wram_reserved` and `sram_reserved` for hot/persistent memory budgets

The trainer's preflight checks expert payload sizes against the smallest ExpertBank slot's usable_bytes, not a hardcoded constant. This automatically adapts as the runtime shell grows or shrinks.

### Why "chrome" in the name

"Chrome" is the browser-industry term for the UI frame around content. Here it means: the runtime shell is the chrome, and the model inference is the content. The budget tells you how much room the content has after the chrome is installed.

### Confidence: HIGH

This is a clean, simple contract that prevents a real failure mode. The only reservation is timing — you need a working runtime shell build before you can emit it, so this becomes practical around M2-M3.

comments.text:
## Architecture decisions from 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session

The model/runtime interface for respecting the GB memory map was reviewed against planv0.md. Four decisions were committed; this comment records them so future agents do not re-litigate.

### 1. Reference runtime shell composition (pinned for pre-training)

The shell used to emit the canonical pre-training `RuntimeChromeBudget` is **minimal + UI**:

- Included: `boot`, `interrupts`, `scheduler`, `banking`, `joypad`, `text`, `keyboard`, `video_commit`.
- **Excluded** from the reference shell: `persistence`, `trace`, `harness`, `panic`.

When any excluded module ships in production, `RuntimeChromeBudget` changes — Bank0Free shrinks, `wram_reserved` and `sram_reserved` grow. Models trained against the current reference shell must be re-preflighted (or retrained) at that point. This is captured in T2.4 (`ReferenceShellSpec` with explicit module set) and T2.5 (CI drift gate on `runtime_nucleus_hash`).

### 2. Slack reservation policy

`reserved_slack` and `wram_reserved` / `sram_reserved` carry **named** reservations for known-future modules so the budget does not silently shift when those modules land. T2.4 defines `ReferenceShellSpec` with a per-module slack table covering `persistence`, `trace`, `harness`, `panic`. The shell emitter records "future-reserved" bytes alongside actually-consumed bytes; preflight subtracts both.

This is option (a) of "reserve named slack now" vs "retrain on shell expansion." The intent is to absorb at least one shell expansion without invalidating training.

### 3. Preflight cadence

Two checkpoints, both required:

- **Pre-training pin.** Trainer captures `runtime_nucleus_hash` from the chosen reference `RuntimeChromeBudget` at training start. Drift mid-training is a hard failure (T2.5 — CI gate).
- **Per-export re-validate.** Export pipeline re-runs preflight against the *current* `RuntimeChromeBudget`. If the production shell has drifted from the training-time reference, export refuses with diagnostic listing both hashes (T2.7).

Pre-runtime training is supported via a deterministic synthetic reference budget under a stable, marked hash (T2.6) so F2 is useful at M1–M2 before a real runtime shell exists.

### 4. `runtime_nucleus_hash` is load-bearing

The hash field in `RuntimeChromeBudget` is the single drift detector between training-time and production-time. Any change to the runtime nucleus binary (or its config) bumps the hash. Drift is gated by CI (T2.5), not informally tracked.

### Cross-references

- WRAM layout (fixed overlay region + ArenaPlan-managed remainder) and the BringUp `CompileProfile` (with hard switch cap) are tracked under F11.
- Sequence-state experimentation (LinearState vs BoundedKv on the same training scaffold) is tracked under F12.
- `wram_reserved: u16` becomes structured `WramReserved { overlay, hot_arena_floor, total }` — see T11.1 and the comment on T2.1.

Production profiles are deferred until F12 sequence-state experimentation resolves.

comments.text:
## F11: CompileProfile + WRAM Layout Contract

### Background

The plan (planv0.md) repeatedly refers to `CompilePreferences`, `BuildConstraints`, and a "WRAM hot arena" without ever pinning down the *layout contract* that bounds them. Two specific gaps surfaced in the 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session:

1. The default residency strategy for expert hot kernels is **WRAM overlay** (per-expert-switch reload). Overlay competes with the hot arena for 8 KiB of WRAM, so the split must be a contract, not a vibe.
2. There is no named `CompileProfile` in the codebase. Preflight, switch-cap enforcement, and policy gating all need named profiles to refer to. Decision from the session: define **`BringUp` only for now**; production profiles wait until F12 (sequence-state experimentation) resolves.

### The problem this solves

Without F11:
- Preflight can't tell the trainer how much WRAM is actually free for the activation/accumulator/scratch arena, because overlay size is implicit.
- The "hard switch cap per profile" decision has nowhere to live. `max_bank_switches_per_token` exists as a `BuildConstraints` field (planv0.md) but is not bound to a named profile.
- `OverlayPlan` (planv0.md compiler-pipeline section 8.5) has no input shape — the compiler can't know how big to size the overlay region.

### Design

Add three policy types to `gbf-policy`:

```rust
pub struct WramLayoutPolicy {
    pub overlay_bytes: u16,
    pub continuation_bytes: u16,
    pub stack_bytes: u16,
    pub hot_arena_bytes_min: u16,
}

pub enum OverlayReloadPolicy {
    PerExpertSwitch,  // default
    PerSlice,
    PerLayerStep,
}

pub struct CompileProfile {
    pub id: CompileProfileId,
    pub wram_layout: WramLayoutPolicy,
    pub overlay_reload: OverlayReloadPolicy,
    pub max_bank_switches_per_token: u16,
    pub sequence_state: SequenceSemanticsRef,
}
```

`RuntimeChromeBudget.wram_reserved` is restructured into `WramReserved { overlay, hot_arena_floor, total }` so preflight can subtract overlay independently from runtime-internal use.

`OverlayPlan` (T11.4) becomes the IR slot that consumes `WramLayoutPolicy.overlay_bytes` and produces a residents list.

### Decisions committed in 2026-04-26 session

- WRAM overlay is the **default** expert-kernel residency strategy.
- Overlay reload policy: **per expert switch**.
- WRAM split: **fixed overlay (per profile constant) + computed remainder via `ArenaPlan`**.
- Switch cap: **hard per-profile constraint**, not just a soft training prior.
- Named profiles: **`BringUp` only**; production profiles deferred until F12 settles.

### Confidence: HIGH for types, MEDIUM for BringUp constants

The type design is straightforward and tightly constrained by F2's existing budget contract. The concrete `BringUp` constants (overlay byte size, switch cap value) are starting hand-picked numbers that calibration (gbf-bench) and shadow_compile data will refine. Provenance must be recorded in T11.2's profile definition.

title: T10.6a: Standard loss term gradient flow tests (z-loss, balance, range, zero)
description:
Per-loss-term gradient flow verification for the standard (non-temporal) loss terms:

**For each of: λ_balance, λ_zrouter, λ_range, λ_zero:**
- Create a tiny model + synthetic input where the term should produce non-zero loss
- Compute the loss term in isolation (all other λ=0)
- Backward pass
- Verify: gradient is non-zero on the expected parameters
- Verify: gradient is zero on parameters the term should NOT affect
- Log: term name, loss value, grad norm per parameter group

**Specific gradient flow tests:**
- λ_balance: gradient should flow only through router logits, NOT through expert weights
- λ_zrouter: gradient should flow only through router logits
- λ_range: gradient through activations at expert boundaries, proportional to distance outside safe range
- λ_zero: gradient through pre-threshold expert weights, proportional to absolute value (L1-style)

**Independence test:**
- Enable each term one at a time, verify total loss equals that term
- Enable all four terms, verify total loss equals sum (linearity)

**Numerical stability tests:**
- Very large router logits (>100): verify z-loss doesn't overflow
- Very small expert usage (one expert gets 99%): verify balance loss is finite and large
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- loss_tests passes; Each loss term has gradient flow test; No NaN/Inf on extreme inputs; Independence verified

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "BoundedKv", "bounded-KV causal", "SramPagePlan". Also F12 (bd-144) and T12.1 (bd-ctl).

### Implementation Notes

Implement a `BoundedKvBlock` that satisfies `SequenceBlock`:

- **State shape**: KV slab sized by `(max_context, kv_bytes_per_token)`. Grows with token position up to `max_context`; bounded above.
- **Update rule**: causal attention with bounded KV (sliding-window or fixed-context truncation). Per-token compute: O(context).
- **Deploy memory**: KV slab is paged through SRAM via `SramPagePlan` (planv0.md compiler-pipeline section 7). Hot tiles get copied into WRAM, worked on there, written back at commit boundaries. This block must not assume the whole KV fits in WRAM.
- **Numerics**: same QAT compatibility as LinearState. Use F1 QAT modules at expert input/output, residual boundaries, and deployable norm.
- **Export facts**: populate `SequenceExportFacts` with `bytes_per_token = kv_bytes_per_token`, `bytes_per_layer = max_context * kv_bytes_per_token`, and the SequenceSemanticsSpec::BoundedKv variant.

### Loss / phase compatibility

The same loss terms (z-loss, balance, range, switch, distillation) and phase scheduler must work without code branching outside the block dispatch — this is the F12 contract. If a loss term needs sequence-state-specific behavior, it goes through a `SequenceBlock`-trait method, not a match statement on the variant.

### What "good enough for v1" looks like

- Tiny tests instantiate it with `max_context=32, kv_bytes_per_token=8`.
- Gradient flows through bounded attention (smoke test).
- ExportVisitor (T1.6) produces CanonicalTensors for its parameters.

comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-artifact`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-artifact/src/weight_plan.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "TernaryWeightPlan", "ScaleGranularity", "ScaleFormat", "ThresholdPlan", "compute_byte_cost".

### Implementation Notes
Define these types:
```rust
pub struct TernaryWeightPlan {
    pub encoding: WeightEncoding,
    pub scale_granularity: ScaleGranularity,
    pub scale_format: ScaleFormat,
    pub threshold: ThresholdPlan,
}

pub enum ScaleGranularity { PerTensor, PerOutputRow, PerGroup(u16) }
pub enum ScaleFormat { Q8_8, Q4_4, Pow2 }
pub enum ThresholdPlan { FixedQ8_8, AnnealedGlobalThenPerOutputRow, LearnedPerGroup(u16) }
```

Key method:
```rust
impl TernaryWeightPlan {
    pub fn compute_byte_cost(&self, rows: u32, cols: u32) -> ByteCost { ... }
}
```

Byte math (from the epic comment):
- Ternary2 base: `ceil(rows * cols / 4)` bytes (2 bits per weight)
- Scale overhead depends on granularity:
  - PerTensor: 2 bytes (one Q8_8 scale)
  - PerOutputRow: `rows * size_of(scale_format)` bytes
  - PerGroup(g): `ceil(rows * cols / g) * size_of(scale_format)` bytes
- Total = ternary_bytes + scale_bytes

Derive: `Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize`

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-artifact                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- weight_plan            # Unit tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-artifact -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: compute_byte_cost returns correct values for known inputs
# Test: PerOutputRow with 64 rows, 128 cols => ternary=2048, scales=128 (Q8_8=2 bytes each -> 64*2=128)
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: ScaleGranularity/ScaleFormat/ThresholdPlan as enums — no stringly-typed configs
- §III.2: Property tests for byte cost monotonicity (more rows/cols => more bytes)

- safe_bound comes from the target's accumulator bit width (e.g., i16 -> 32767 scaled down by accumulator chain length).
- Applied at expert input/output boundaries — works alongside ActFakeQuant.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::range          # Tests pass
# Verify: activations within safe_bound -> penalty = 0
# Verify: activations exceeding bound -> quadratic penalty
# Verify: gradient is zero within bound, linear outside bound
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Pure function
- §I.5 (Honest Loss): This term is differentiable w.r.t. activations — a legitimate training signal

title: F9: Adaptive Expert Shapes — M6 Research Mode via ExpertShapePolicy
description: ExpertShapePolicy::StructuredWidthGates allows training experts as a supernet and exporting after hardening/pruning into bank-fitting widths. This is where lambda_shape/lambda_overflow become meaningful. M6-scoped research mode, not part of the default training path.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F9: Adaptive Expert Shapes — M6 Research Mode via ExpertShapePolicy

### Background

The default training path uses fixed-shape experts: d_model -> d_ff -> d_model with fixed dimensions chosen before training. The byte cost is deterministic from the architecture parameters + TernaryWeightPlan, and bank fit is verified at preflight time. This is simple, predictable, and sufficient for M0-M5.

However, there's a research question: could experts learn to be different sizes? Some experts might need more capacity (wider d_ff) while others could be smaller. If the system could learn per-expert widths and then prune/harden them to fit banks, it might achieve better quality for the same total ROM budget.

### ExpertShapePolicy

- Fixed: all experts have the same d_ff. Default. Used M0-M5.
- StructuredWidthGates { row_group, col_group }: experts are trained as a supernet with max d_ff, but each row_group x col_group block has a learnable gate. During training, gates close gradually (annealing). At export, closed gates are pruned and the expert is exported with its actual (possibly smaller) width. This is the regime where λ_shape and λ_overflow become meaningful training signals.

### Why this is M6-scoped

1. It requires a working fixed-shape training pipeline first (M0-M5 worth of infrastructure)
2. The quality benefit is uncertain — it might not be worth the complexity
3. The compiler needs to handle variable-width experts (different byte costs per expert, different bank packing)
4. The training dynamics of width gates + ternary quantization + routing are not well-studied

### Why it's tracked now despite being M6

The plan's loss function (F5) includes λ_shape and λ_overflow terms that are gated on this enum. The enum needs to exist (even if only the Fixed variant is implemented) so that the gating logic is correct from the start. Adding it later would require touching the loss function config, the model config, the export logic, and the compiler's budget calculations.

### Confidence: LOW-MEDIUM

This is the most speculative feature in the entire training-contract revision. The Fixed variant is trivial and definitely correct. The StructuredWidthGates variant is a research idea that may or may not pan out. The value of tracking it now is: (a) it makes the λ_shape/λ_overflow gating logic explicit, (b) it leaves a clean extension point for M6, (c) it costs almost nothing to define the enum with one variant now.

description: Nine training-contract corrections that transform the plan's training/model side from hand-wavy aspirations into implementable engineering. Covers: Burn reframing, RuntimeChromeBudget, TernaryWeightPlan, phased QAT training with dense teacher, honest loss function, MoE architecture restrictions, router switch-awareness, shadow export/compile, and M6 adaptive expert shapes.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Training-Contract Revision Pass — Master Tracking Issue

### What this is

The architecture plan (planv0.md) was comprehensive on the compiler, runtime, oracle, and artifact boundary sides but left the training/model side underspecified. This epic tracks nine corrections that make the training contract implementable rather than aspirational.

### Why this matters

Without these corrections, the first person to actually implement training would hit a cascade of unresolved questions:
- "Burn owns QAT" — but Burn doesn't have QAT. Who owns the ternary projection code?
- "Bank-friendly expert sizing" — but the loss function penalizes bank overflow for fixed-shape experts where bank overflow is a geometry property, not a differentiable training signal.
- "Use 16 KiB experts" — but 16 KiB of what? Raw ternary bits? Plus scales? Plus metadata? The byte formula was never specified.
- "Router locality loss" — but locality of what kind? Spatial? Temporal? The switch cost on GB is primarily temporal (consecutive tokens hitting different banks).
- "Export a ReferenceModelBundle" — from what? The full-precision model? A dense teacher? When in training?

### The nine corrections

1. **Burn as front-end, not numerics owner** — Burn provides autodiff/optimizers/checkpoints; gbf-model owns deployed numerics (ternary projection, activation fake-quant, norm approximation, export visitation)
2. **RuntimeChromeBudget** — The trainer needs to know how many bytes are free in each bank class AFTER the runtime shell is built, not just a static 16 KiB constant
3. **TernaryWeightPlan** — WeightEncoding::Ternary2 alone doesn't specify scale granularity, scale format, threshold schedule, or exported byte cost
4. **Phased training with dense teacher** — Training doesn't go from random init to hard ternary in one shot; there's a 5-phase schedule with a frozen dense teacher for distillation and denotational reference
5. **Honest loss function** — The old λ_bank/λ_locality terms were phantom gradients for fixed-shape experts; replaced with terms that are actually differentiable w.r.t. the quantities they claim to optimize
6. **MoE architecture restrictions** — MoE on FFN path only, two-matrix experts (not GLU), tied embeddings, optional shared dense branch, selected blocks not every block
7. **Router switch-awareness** — Temporal switch cost is the real deployment bottleneck; router training gets z-loss, expert dropout, temporal smoothness regularization, and TemporalSwitchDigest in export facts
8. **Shadow export/compile during training** — Don't wait until end of training to discover the model doesn't fit; periodic EMA export + compile + conformance check with Pareto frontier selection
9. **Adaptive expert shapes (M6)** — StructuredWidthGates as an explicit M6 research mode where λ_shape/λ_overflow actually become meaningful

### Dependency structure

The features form a DAG:
- F1 (Burn reframing) is the foundation — almost everything depends on gbf-model owning the QAT modules
- F6 (MoE arch) is structurally independent but defines the model that F3/F5/F7 must support
- F3 (TernaryWeightPlan) depends on F1
- F7 (Router switch-awareness) depends on F6
- F4 (Phased training) depends on F1 and F3
- F5 (Loss function) depends on F3 and F7
- F2 (RuntimeChromeBudget) has external deps on gbf-hw/gbf-policy but no internal feature deps
- F8 (Shadow compile) depends on F2 and F4
- F9 (Adaptive shapes) depends on F3 and F5, is M6-deferred

### Milestone mapping

- M1-M2: F1 (Burn reframing), F3 (TernaryWeightPlan basics), F6 (MoE arch decisions)
- M3: F6 (implementation), F7 (router switch-awareness)
- M3-M4: F4 (phased training), F2 (RuntimeChromeBudget)
- M4-M5: F8 (shadow compile)
- M6: F5 (full loss function), F9 (adaptive shapes)

### Assessment notes

Revisions 1, 3, 5, 8, 2 are wholeheartedly endorsed — they fix genuine holes that would cause implementation failures. Revisions 4 and 6-7 are endorsed with mild reservations about specificity (the exact phase ordering and block selection may need revision once training data exists). Revision 9 is the most speculative — it's harmless but premature as a first-class type.

This epic now includes two additional features that emerged from the session reviewing how the model/runtime interface respects the Game Boy memory map:

- **F11: CompileProfile + WRAM Layout Contract** — defines `WramLayoutPolicy`, `OverlayReloadPolicy`, the `CompileProfile` registry, and the named `BringUp` profile (single profile for now; production profiles deferred until F12 experimentation resolves). comments.text:
## F10: Training-Contract Test Suite and Diagnostic Logging

### Why this feature exists

The training-contract revision adds 47 implementation tasks across 9 features. Without a corresponding test suite, the first integration attempt will surface all the subtle disagreements between features simultaneously — a debugging nightmare. Each feature's contracts (type invariants, numeric agreements, gradient flow, byte formulas) need to be verified independently before integration.

### Test philosophy

1. **Tiny fixtures**: All tests use a micro MoE model (2 experts, d_model=8, d_ff=16, 2 layers, vocab=32). This trains in seconds on CPU and exercises every code path. Real-scale testing happens in gbf-bench, not here.

2. **Layered testing**: Unit tests verify individual modules in isolation. Integration tests verify feature interactions. E2E tests verify the full pipeline. Each layer catches different classes of bugs.

3. **Diagnostic output**: Every test emits structured logs that can be used to diagnose failures. Test failures include: which assertion failed, what the expected vs actual values were, the full state context (model config, TernaryWeightPlan, phase, step), and suggested investigation steps.

4. **Regression safety**: Snapshot/golden tests for byte formulas, export formats, and artifact hashes prevent silent regressions when any component changes.

### Test task dependency structure

- T10.1 (fixtures) and T10.2 (logging) have no deps — they're infrastructure
- Unit test tasks (T10.3-T10.10) each depend on the implementation tasks they test + T10.1
- Integration tests (T10.11-T10.12) depend on unit tests passing first
- E2E tests (T10.13-T10.15) depend on integration tests
- The regression script (T10.15) depends on everything

### Logging architecture

Structured logging using `tracing` crate with:
- Span-per-phase (DenseTeacherWarmup, RouterWarmup, etc.)
- Per-step loss term breakdown (each λ term logged individually)
- Phase transition events with before/after QAT hardness
- Export events with artifact hash, byte counts, TernaryWeightPlan summary
- Shadow compile events with fit/fail status, frontier point details
- Preflight events with per-check pass/fail and detailed diagnostics

All events use structured key-value fields, not free-form strings, so they can be queried programmatically in CI.

title: T0.3: Define core shared types in gbf-foundation
description:
Define the core shared types that many crates reference but nobody explicitly creates. Without these, downstream tasks (T1.2, T3.1, T6.1, T7.1, T2.1, etc.) will each need to invent their own placeholder types, leading to inconsistency.

Types to define in gbf-foundation/src/lib.rs (or submodules):
- Hash256: [u8; 32] newtype with hex display, from_bytes, as_bytes
- ByteCost: u64 newtype with Display, Add, Sub, comparison
- TargetProfileId, CompileProfileId, TargetFamilyId: typed string wrappers
- LayerId, ExpertId: u16 newtypes with From<u16>
- BudgetSlotId: typed id
- CheckpointId, WorkloadId, CalibrationSetRef ids
- SemVer: simple semver struct
- Common derives: Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize on all

These are deliberately minimal newtypes. The point is to prevent raw u16/String misuse across crate boundaries (CONSTITUTION §I.1: make invalid states unrepresentable).

title: T10.13: Export determinism and cross-backend agreement test
description:
Tests that the export pipeline produces byte-identical artifacts for the same model weights.

**Determinism test:**
- Create a tiny model with fixed random seed
- Export via ExportVisitor -> ModelArtifact, record ArtifactCore content hash H1
- Reset model to same weights (reload from checkpoint)
- Export again -> record hash H2
- ASSERT: H1 == H2

**Serialization round-trip test:**
- Export ModelArtifact to directory form
- Read it back via artifact loader
- Re-export to directory form
- Verify byte-for-byte identical files in blobs/sha256/

**Canonical tensor determinism:**
- For each CanonicalTensor in ArtifactCore: serialize to bytes, hash, deserialize, re-serialize, verify hash unchanged
- Includes ternary weight tensors, scale tensors, embedding tensors, router weights

**Production packer vs independent reference packer:**
- Export tiny model via production ExportVisitor
- For each ternary weight tensor: repack using gbf-verify's independent reference packer
- ASSERT: byte-identical packed output for all tensors
- If ANY diverge: log tensor name, first divergent byte offset, hex context around divergence

**Scale tensor quantization determinism:**
- Export scale tensors in Q8_8 format
- Verify the quantized scale values match: round_to_nearest(float_scale * 256) as u16
- Verify no NaN or Inf values in exported scales

All failures log: artifact hash, tensor name, byte offset of divergence, hex dump.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- export_determinism passes; Hash equality across runs; Serialization round-trip byte-identical; 100 random packer agreement tests

title: T7.5: Implement low-rank router projection
description: Factor the router's d_model -> n_experts projection as two matrices: d_model -> rank and rank -> n_experts. Default rank = min(n_experts/4, 8). For d_model=128, n_experts=16, rank=4: saves ~1.5 KiB of common-bank space. The low-rank projection also acts as implicit regularization, preventing the router from memorizing token-expert assignments. Both matrices stay at higher precision (not ternary) since they're small and live in common banks.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Refinement from 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session

### Two preflight cadences (both required)

Preflight against `RuntimeChromeBudget` runs at two distinct points; do not collapse to one.

**A. Training-start pin.**
- `gbf-train` captures the active `RuntimeChromeBudget` (real or synthetic per T2.6) at training start.
- Records `runtime_nucleus_hash` and `reference_shell_modules` into the run manifest.
- Subsequent training steps assert the captured hash is unchanged (T2.5 enforces this in CI for the production shell, but the training run also self-checks).
- Hash drift mid-training = hard failure; trainer refuses to continue. The fix is either (i) re-pin to the new hash and accept the budget shift, or (ii) revert the runtime shell change.

**B. Per-export re-validation.**
- Export pipeline (driven from F4 `HardenAndSelect` / F8 shadow_compile) re-runs the same preflight against the **current** `RuntimeChromeBudget`.
- Diagnostic on hash drift names both hashes and the per-slot byte deltas:
  ```
  Pre-training: runtime_nucleus_hash=0xAB12... ExpertBank.usable_bytes=15488
  At export:    runtime_nucleus_hash=0xCD34... ExpertBank.usable_bytes=15104
  Delta: -384 bytes per ExpertBank (persistence module added).
  ```
- Re-validation failure on hard fits (`fits_envelope=false`) blocks export. Risk-only flags are surfaced but not blocking.

T2.7 owns the per-export re-validation step; T2.3 (this task) owns the training-start pin and the re-runnable preflight implementation that both cadences share.

### What the preflight checks against `RuntimeChromeBudget`

- Per-expert payload bytes ≤ `min(ExpertBank.usable_bytes − reserved_slack)` across `ExpertBank` slots.
- Shared kernel + embedding + router + classifier total ≤ sum of `CommonBank.usable_bytes`.
- Hot kernels needing Bank0 residency ≤ `Bank0Free.usable_bytes − reserved_slack`.
- Per-token sequence-state bytes ≤ `sram_reserved` budget.
- Hot-arena demand from `ArenaPlan` ≤ `wram_reserved.hot_arena_floor`.
- Required overlay payload size ≤ `wram_reserved.overlay` (the per-`CompileProfile` overlay constant, see F11/T11.1).
- `CompileProfile.max_bank_switches_per_token` is satisfiable given declared MoE topology + tile plan (this is a soft check pre-training, hard check at export when scheduling exists).

### Diagnostic format

Existing diagnostic stays. Add a `WramFitReport` block:
```
WRAM split: overlay=512, hot_arena_floor=4096, total reserved=4608.
ArenaPlan demand: hot_arena=3712 -> PASS with 384 bytes margin.
Overlay fit: largest expert kernel=380 bytes -> PASS with 132 bytes margin.
```

And a `SwitchBudgetReport` block once F11/T11.x lands.

title: F8: Shadow Export/Compile During Training with Pareto Selection
description: On selected checkpoints during training, export EMA weights, compile under one or more CompileRequests, run a small conformance suite, and track a Pareto frontier of quality/fit/cost. Prevents end-of-training surprises. Defines ShadowCompilePolicy and CheckpointFrontierPoint.
acceptance_criteria: All T8.x tasks closed; Shadow compile pipeline tested E2E; Pareto selection tested

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F8: Shadow Export/Compile During Training with Pareto Selection

### Background

A common failure mode in ML systems: you train a model for days/weeks, export it, try to compile/deploy, and discover it doesn't fit the target constraints. For this project, the failure modes include:
- Expert weights that are technically ternary but whose scales push the byte count over the bank budget
- Activation ranges that break the compiler's integer accumulator plan
- A routing pattern that causes pathological bank-switch counts
- A model that passes all quality metrics but whose scheduled execution exceeds the cycle budget

### The solution: compile-in-the-loop

During training (specifically during phases C-E), periodically:
1. Export the current EMA weights as a ModelArtifact
2. Compile under one or more CompileRequests (at least Default profile)
3. Run a small artifact/schedule conformance suite (a few workloads, not the full suite)
4. Record the result as a CheckpointFrontierPoint: quality + conformance + projected fit + schedule cost
5. Track a Pareto frontier of the best checkpoints across the quality/fit/cost dimensions

### ShadowCompilePolicy

Controls the shadow compile frequency and scope:
- every_n_steps: how often to run shadow compile (default: every 1000 steps during Phase E)
- requests: which CompileRequests to test (default: just Default profile)
- workloads: which WorkloadManifests to run conformance against (default: fixtures/workloads/smoke.toml)
- keep_frontier: how many frontier points to keep (default: 10)

### CheckpointFrontierPoint

Records the result of each shadow compile:
- checkpoint: which training checkpoint was exported
- quality: validation loss, perplexity, or other quality metrics
- conformance: denotational-vs-artifact conformance envelope summary
- projected_fit: does it fit the RuntimeChromeBudget? any hard failures?
- schedule_cost: estimated cycles/token, bank-switches/token from ScheduleCostAnalysis

### The training_selection.json artifact

At the end of training (or when manually triggered), the frontier selection algorithm picks the best checkpoint by:
1. Filter: remove any point with hard fit failures
2. Pareto: find points that aren't dominated on (quality, conformance, cost)
3. Select: among Pareto-optimal points, pick the one with the best quality

This checkpoint is then fully exported as the final ModelArtifact + ReferenceModelBundle.

### Practical considerations

Shadow compile is expensive. For a small model it might take seconds; for a larger one, minutes. Running it every training step is infeasible. The every_n_steps parameter should be set so that shadow compile adds < 5% to total training time. During Phase E (HardenAndSelect), this is tightened because that phase is specifically about selection.

The shadow compile can also feed compiler_feedback.json back into the training loop's hint bundle, closing the bidirectional feedback loop described in the plan.

### Confidence: HIGH

This is the single best practical reliability improvement. Finding deployment failures at training time instead of after training saves enormous amounts of time. The types (ShadowCompilePolicy, CheckpointFrontierPoint) are clean and general.

title: F5: Honest Loss Function — Only Penalize What Is Differentiable
description: Replace the old lambda_bank/lambda_locality phantom-gradient terms with an honest loss: lambda_distill, lambda_balance, lambda_zrouter, lambda_switch, lambda_range, lambda_zero. Gate lambda_shape/lambda_overflow on ExpertShapePolicy since they are meaningless for fixed-shape Ternary2. M6-scoped.
acceptance_criteria: All T5.x tasks closed; All loss terms tested with gradient flow verification

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F5: Honest Loss Function — Only Penalize What Is Differentiable

### Background

The original plan included:
```
loss = lm_loss + λ_zero * sparsity_regularizer + λ_bank * expert_budget_penalty + λ_locality * router_locality_penalty + λ_range * activation_range_penalty
```

This looks reasonable but is mathematically dishonest in two ways:

1. **λ_bank * expert_budget_penalty**: For fixed-shape Ternary2 experts (the default), the byte cost of an expert is `ceil(rows*cols/4) + scale_bytes + metadata_bytes`. This is a function of the architecture dimensions, NOT of the learned weights. No gradient can flow through it. The penalty is a constant during training — it's either satisfied or not at architecture-definition time. Making it a loss term is cargo-cult optimization.

2. **λ_locality * router_locality_penalty**: "Locality" is vague. Spatial locality (nearby tokens use nearby experts)? Temporal locality (consecutive tokens reuse the same expert)? The deployment cost is temporal — a bank switch happens when token t routes to a different expert than token t-1 in the same layer. The old term didn't specify which kind.

### The corrected loss

```
loss = lm_loss + λ_distill * logit_distillation_loss + λ_balance * expert_load_balance_loss + λ_zrouter * router_z_loss + λ_switch * temporal_switch_penalty + λ_range * activation_range_penalty + λ_zero * ternary_zero_regularizer + λ_shape * structured_shape_penalty + λ_overflow * bank_overflow_penalty
```

Each term is honest about what it can influence:

- **λ_distill**: KL divergence from frozen dense teacher — always meaningful during QAT phases
- **λ_balance**: Standard expert load-balance loss (entropy-based) — prevents expert collapse
- **λ_zrouter**: Router z-loss (penalizes large logits before softmax) — stabilizes training
- **λ_switch**: L_switch = (1/LT) Σ_{l,t} (1 - ⟨p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1}⟩) — directly penalizes the deployment cost (bank switches), and the gradient flows through the router
- **λ_range**: Penalizes activations outside the safe integer range — gradient flows through the activations
- **λ_zero**: Penalizes non-zero ternary weights — gradient flows through the pre-threshold weights
- **λ_shape** (DISABLED for fixed-shape): Penalizes non-bank-fitting expert widths — only meaningful for StructuredWidthGates
- **λ_overflow** (DISABLED for fixed-shape): Penalizes total byte cost exceeding bank capacity — only meaningful when byte cost depends on learned structure

### The temporal switch penalty specifically

L_switch = (1/LT) Σ_{l,t} (1 - ⟨p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1}⟩)

where p_{l,t} is the soft expert distribution for layer l, token t. When consecutive tokens pick the same expert, the inner product is 1 and the penalty is 0. When they pick different experts, the inner product decreases and the penalty increases. This is a differentiable proxy for the actual bank-switch count that will be seen at deployment.

### M6 scope

All these loss terms are M6-scoped except λ_balance and λ_zrouter which are standard MoE training practice and should be included from Phase B onward. The other terms are tuning features that require a working model + compiler to evaluate.

### Confidence: VERY HIGH

The mathematical argument is airtight. The old loss had phantom gradients; the new one doesn't.

- T5.6 (loss config, P2): Config infrastructure for all loss terms. Also M3-level. Ready NOW.

title: T9.2: Implement StructuredWidthGates supernet training (M6)
description: M6 research: train experts with max d_ff, but each (row_group x col_group) block has a learnable sigmoid gate. During training, the gate values are annealed from 1.0 toward 0.0 using a temperature schedule. The lambda_shape penalty encourages gates to close (producing sparser experts). The lambda_overflow penalty penalizes total exported bytes exceeding the bank budget. The gradient flows through the gate values and the STE-projected ternary weights.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check passes; Gates are learnable parameters; Sigmoid annealing sharpens over steps; Pruning produces valid smaller experts

title: T10.8: Router and temporal smoothness tests (F7 coverage)
description:
Unit tests for router training features:

**Temporal smoothness boundary handling:**
- Sequence of 8 tokens, all routing to expert 0: verify L_switch = 0
- Sequence of 8 tokens, alternating expert 0 and 1: verify L_switch ≈ 1.0
- Two sequences in same batch (tokens [0-3] = seq A, [4-7] = seq B): verify no cross-sequence penalty
- First token of each sequence: verify no penalty (no previous token to compare against)
- Gradient: verify gradient flows through the router softmax for tokens 1+ but not token 0 of each sequence

**Expert dropout tests:**
- Set dropout rate 0.5, run forward 100 times: verify each expert is dropped ~50% of the time (within 3σ)
- Dropout rate 0.0: verify no experts dropped
- Dropout disabled at eval time: verify eval() forward never drops experts
- Gradient through dropped experts: verify gradient is zero for dropped, non-zero for active

**Low-rank factorization tests:**
- Full-rank router (d_model=8 -> n_experts=4): compute output
- Low-rank router (d_model=8 -> rank=2 -> n_experts=4): verify shape is correct
- Verify low-rank param count < full-rank param count
- Gradient flow: verify gradients reach both low-rank matrices

**Temporal switch digest collection:**
- Run model on tiny corpus, collect TemporalSwitchDigest
- Verify same_expert_rate is between 0 and 256 (Q8.8 format)
- Verify transition_mass entries sum to ≤ 256
- Verify n_layers digests are produced

All tests log: n_experts, routing distribution per token, switch rate, dropout mask when applicable.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F6: MoE Architecture Restrictions — Make the First Model Concrete

### Background

The plan describes an MoE (Mixture of Experts) architecture but leaves several critical decisions underspecified:
- Which blocks have MoE? All of them? Some?
- What's the expert internal structure? Two-matrix (standard MLP) or three-matrix (GLU)?
- Are embeddings and classifier separate or tied?
- Is the sequence-state path (attention/recurrent) also MoE, or shared?

These aren't tuning knobs — they fundamentally change the byte budget, the bank layout, the routing semantics, and the training dynamics.

### Key decisions and their justifications

**MoE on FFN path only, not sequence-state**: The sequence-state update (attention, recurrent, linear-attention) is the most numerically sensitive part of the model. It benefits from shared, higher-precision weights in common banks. Making it MoE would mean routing for both FFN and state update, doubling the router complexity and bank-switch cost. The FFN path is the natural place for MoE because experts are relatively independent computations.

**Alternating or middle blocks, not every block**: The first and last blocks tend to learn more universal features (embedding projection, pre-classifier features). MoE in these blocks wastes bank capacity on experts that all produce similar output. Starting MoE at block 2 and using it in every other block (or only in the middle N blocks) is standard practice in efficient MoE designs.

**Two-matrix experts, not GLU**: A GLU expert has three projections: gate, up, and down. For bank-sized ternary:
- Two-matrix (128→224→128): ~15 KiB → fits in one bank with margin
- GLU (128→224→128 with separate gate): ~22.5 KiB → does NOT fit in one bank
The three-matrix GLU would require either larger banks (not available), smaller d_ff (quality hit), or packed multi-expert banks (complexity). Two-matrix with a simple clipped nonlinearity (ReLU, GELU clip) is the right default.

**Tied input/output embeddings**: For a char/byte-level model with a small vocabulary (128-256 tokens), the embedding matrix is tiny (~32-64 KiB). Tying input and output embeddings halves this already-small cost and is standard practice for character models. The embedding lives in a common bank.

**Optional shared dense FFN branch**: A stability technique where each MoE block also has a tiny shared (non-expert) dense FFN in common banks. The sparse expert output is added to the shared output as a delta. This improves training stability because the shared branch provides a stable gradient path even when routing is noisy. It costs a few KiB of common-bank space but may significantly improve quality.

### Confidence: HIGH with mild reservation

The byte math is solid. The "alternating blocks" recommendation is well-supported by the literature but the specific block selection will depend on the model's actual size and the quality/latency experiments that happen during training. The types and structure are right; the defaults may shift.

title: T10.6b: Temporal switch loss gradient flow tests (L_switch)
description:
Gradient flow tests specifically for the temporal switch penalty L_switch:

**Basic gradient flow:**
- Sequence of 8 tokens, all routing to expert 0: verify L_switch = 0, gradient is zero
- Sequence of 8 tokens, alternating expert 0 and 1: verify L_switch ≈ 1.0, gradient is non-zero on router
- Gradient flows through router softmax for tokens 1+ but NOT for token 0 (no previous token)

**Sequence boundary handling:**
- Two sequences in same batch (tokens [0-3] = seq A, [4-7] = seq B): verify NO cross-sequence penalty
- First token of each sequence: verify zero penalty contribution
- Contiguous window size=4: verify penalty computed only within windows

**Interaction with z-loss:**
- Enable both λ_switch and λ_zrouter: verify gradients combine correctly (router receives gradients from both terms)
- The z-loss should soften routing distributions, which should reduce the temporal switch penalty — verify this qualitative behavior on a short training run

**λ_distill interaction:**
- Enable λ_switch + λ_distill: verify student is pushed toward both teacher quality AND temporal smoothness
- If teacher has high switch rate and student learns smooth routing, verify conformance gap is logged

All tests log: n_tokens, n_experts, routing distributions, L_switch value, gradient norms per router parameter.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- switch_loss_tests passes; Gradient encourages same-expert routing; Sequence boundary handling correct

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy` (types) + `gbf-runtime` (consumer).
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/reference_shell.rs` (new), `gbf-runtime/src/lib.rs` (record present modules).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "runtime nucleus", "Bank0", "minimal", "chrome". Also bd-1hv 2026-04-26 session comment, T2.2 refinement (bd-1g9).

### Implementation Notes

Define `ReferenceShellSpec` and the `RuntimeShellModule` enum (the latter is referenced by `RuntimeChromeBudget.reference_shell_modules` per T2.1 refinement):

```rust
pub enum RuntimeShellModule {
    Boot,
    Interrupts,
    Scheduler,
    Banking,
    Joypad,
    Text,
    Keyboard,
    VideoCommit,
    Panic,
    Trace,
    Harness,
    Persistence,
}

pub struct ReferenceShellSpec {
    pub included: BTreeSet<RuntimeShellModule>,
    pub future_reservations: BTreeMap<RuntimeShellModule, FutureReservation>,
}

pub struct FutureReservation {
    pub rom_bytes_per_bank0: u16,
    pub wram_bytes: u16,
    pub sram_bytes: u32,
    pub justification: &'static str,
}

pub fn pinned_reference_shell() -> ReferenceShellSpec {
    use RuntimeShellModule::*;
    ReferenceShellSpec {
        included: [Boot, Interrupts, Scheduler, Banking, Joypad, Text,
                   Keyboard, VideoCommit].into_iter().collect(),
        future_reservations: [
            (Persistence, FutureReservation {
                rom_bytes_per_bank0: 768,    // SRAM record protocol code + headers
                wram_bytes: 64,               // active page header cache in HRAM-adjacent WRAM
                sram_bytes: 512,              // per-page commit metadata
                justification: "Reserve for SRAM record protocol per planv0.md \
                                Persistent record protocol section.",
            }),
            (Trace, FutureReservation {
                rom_bytes_per_bank0: 256,
                wram_bytes: 0,
                sram_bytes: 4096,
                justification: "Dev trace pages live primarily in SRAM.",
            }),
            (Harness, FutureReservation {
                rom_bytes_per_bank0: 384,
                wram_bytes: 0,
                sram_bytes: 256,              // command/result blocks
                justification: "Harness command/result blocks per gbf-abi.",
            }),
            (Panic, FutureReservation {
                rom_bytes_per_bank0: 192,
                wram_bytes: 16,
                sram_bytes: 0,
                justification: "Panic screen + panic ISR landing pad.",
            }),
        ].into_iter().collect(),
    }
}
```

The shell emitter (T2.2) reads `pinned_reference_shell()`, subtracts each `FutureReservation`'s bytes from the relevant `RomBudgetSlot.usable_bytes` / `wram_reserved` / `sram_reserved`, and records the breakdown in `BuildReports`.

### Concrete byte values

The numbers above are starting estimates. They MUST appear in `BuildReports` so reviewers can see what is being reserved and why. They can be refined when each module is actually implemented and its real footprint becomes measurable.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy                                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- reference_shell                      # pinned_reference_shell() returns expected set
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings                          # No warnings
# Verify: pinned_reference_shell().included == minimal+UI set
# Verify: pinned_reference_shell().future_reservations covers Persistence, Trace, Harness, Panic
# Verify: every FutureReservation has a non-empty justification
# Verify: no reservation present for an `included` module
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `RuntimeShellModule` enum is exhaustive; missing a module is a compile error at consumers.
- §VI.1: Reference shell module set lives in code (gbf-policy), not in a config file.
- §V.3: Slack reservations carry justifications so failures can name them.

### Implementation Notes
- Canonical expert byte formula for the default two-matrix Ternary2 PerOutputRow Q8_8 expert:
```rust
pub fn compute_expert_bytes(plan: &TernaryWeightPlan, d_model: u32, d_ff: u32) -> ByteCost {
    let up = plan.compute_byte_cost(d_model, d_ff);   // d_model -> d_ff
    let down = plan.compute_byte_cost(d_ff, d_model);  // d_ff -> d_model
    up + down  // plus any metadata overhead
}
```
- Must be **consistent** with `TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost()` — uses it, doesn't reimplement it.
- Include metadata overhead estimate (tile headers, alignment padding).
- This function is used by both the trainer (preflight) and compiler (StaticBudgetReport).

## Agent Instructions

### Implementation Notes

1. **Scaffold dispatches via `SequenceBlock` trait, not variant matching.**
   - `gbf-train` phase scheduler, loss terms, QAT module wiring, and checkpoint orchestration all call methods on a `dyn SequenceBlock` (or generic `SequenceBlock`) — not `match` on `SequenceSemanticsSpec`.
   - This is the testable contract: a grep for `SequenceSemanticsSpec::` outside `gbf-model` should return zero matches in training code paths.

2. **shadow_compile runs both variants.**
   - `ShadowCompilePolicy.requests` (planv0.md F8) is extended to optionally carry a `dual_sequence_state: bool` flag (or two parallel `CompileRequest`s). When enabled, every selected checkpoint is exported and compiled twice — once as LinearState, once as BoundedKv — under matching `CompileProfile`s (BringUp for both during experimentation).
   - Both produce `CheckpointFrontierPoint`s.

3. **`SequenceStateComparisonReport`.**

   ```rust
   pub struct SequenceStateComparisonReport {
       pub workload: WorkloadId,
       pub linear_state: SequenceVariantSummary,
       pub bounded_kv: SequenceVariantSummary,
       pub generated_at: DateTime<Utc>,
   }

   pub struct SequenceVariantSummary {
       pub quality: QualitySummary,
       pub schedule_cost: Option<EstimatedCostDelta>,
       pub projected_bank_switches_per_token: f32,
       pub projected_state_bytes: u32,
       pub fits_envelope: bool,
   }
   ```

   - Emitted under `artifacts/runs/<run_id>/sequence_state_comparison.json`.
   - Acts as the empirical input that justifies defining `Production-LinearState` or `Production-BoundedKv` `CompileProfile`s later.

4. **No production CompileProfile is created by this task.**
   - That decision waits for the report to actually contain data on a real workload. T12.4 ends when the report is generated for at least one tiny-model run.

cargo test -p gbf-train -- scaffold::dispatch                   # Scaffold uses trait, not variant match
cargo test -p gbf-train -- shadow::dual_path                    # Both variants run on a tiny model
cargo test -p gbf-test -- sequence_state_comparison_report      # Report shape stable
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings                         # No warnings

# Verify: shadow_compile run produces sequence_state_comparison.json with both variants populated
# Verify: report fits_envelope is computed against the BringUp profile and pinned RuntimeChromeBudget
